text: does anyone know of any radical paradigms for navigating the web? eg: not treating web pages like documents, not treating history as mostly ephemeral, facilitating collection or breadth-first search, weird zooming user interfaces, etc?

does anyone know of any radical paradigms for navigating the web? eg: not treating web pages like documents, not treating history as mostly ephemeral, facilitating collection or breadth-first search, weird zooming user interfaces, etc?
That makes sense. Thanks!
That makes sense. Thanks!

The page-centric concept of the web (from a browser standpoint) is a bit strange to begin with, if you think about it. I think it would make sense if instead of "loading a page", you loaded a script, which then programmatically creates content. And then, why should loading something else have to make that go away? Perhaps loading the same thing twice would just give you two copies of it, just like if you run the same program twice or open the same file twice. That would also give "history" a different connotation

Of course, it would be a mess if everything just populated into the same user-side space, so perhaps a script would get it's own bounded view and execution sandbox, and could be allowed to load other scripts into it. And then you could wipe out a whole context if you wanted ... Aaaand I think I just described actual webpages again :/

You should look at Ted Nelson's book "Dream Machines" (1974). Nelson coined the word hypertext and invented the world wide web in the late 1960's/early 1970's. The web browser paradigm we use today was first implemented by Nelson and others as "The Hypertext Editing System" at Brown University, in the late 1960's. Nelson considered it a failed prototype, and went on to design a much better system, called Xanadu, which is documented in that book.

text: My own <http://Object.Network> design reinvents the web in a way: instead of HTML docs linked together, which assumes presentation level, with maybe some semantics piggy-backed on or tunnelled through, it has what I've unimaginatively called "objects" linked together. Like our familiar OO objects, these can be both data and docs, "semantic" or "model" as well as "view".

My own <http://Object.Network> design reinvents the web in a way: instead of HTML docs linked together, which assumes presentation level, with maybe some semantics piggy-backed on or tunnelled through, it has what I've unimaginatively called "objects" linked together. Like our familiar OO objects, these can be both data and docs, "semantic" or "model" as well as "view".

So, to more directly answer the OP's challenge: in the Object Net you can navigate through the network (web) of objects by transclusion or by jumping to linked objects, just like in the web.

So the interface is a 2D view of nested boxes each representing an object you can see from the current object you're on, via links, up to a finite depth. So for example you get the "mirrors facing each other" effect if two objects point to each other and you keep opening each up inside the other.

So, to more directly answer the OP's challenge: in the Object Net you can navigate through the network (web) of objects by transclusion or by jumping to linked objects, just like in the web.

So the interface is a 2D view of nested boxes each representing an object you can see from the current object you're on, via links, up to a finite depth. So for example you get the "mirrors facing each other" effect if two objects point to each other and you keep opening each up inside the other.
If an object happens to be a "view" or document or presentational object that the "browser" recognises, it can be rendered as the fully painted thing, not the raw data.

Hi Steve. And thanks! :slightly_smiling_face: Because I have used io a long time ago, I love it and it made me discovered prototype based languages. In fact this is precisely what made me reconsider JavaScript. At that time, I thought JavaScript was a very basic toy language not very enjoyable and when I discover prototype in io and then understood that JavaScript has it also, I view it as an interesting language to use and actually move in learning it and appreciate it for its "good parts". So thanks again! :slightly_smiling_face:

And I just discover your work on voluntary which seems very interesting too! I was looking into a kind of p2p communication in the browser for some experiments on offline first collaborative editing (based on Causal Tree/CRDT). My need is sending messages (actually, editing events) between peers. Each message must be associated to each person/editor (one person can possibly have several editors openned at the same time). Do you think the p2p layer of voluntary can be a good fit for this kind of use?

Hi Nicolas, yes, voluntary's messaging layer was designed to be a single p2p messaging for any/all apps that would like to use it. The messages sent are just json (with a standard encryption and signature format) which contain a payload that declares it's type and can contain anything).

Beyond that, my best advice would be to just hang out on this Slack for a while. A lot of the big, useful ideas come up again and again as we explore them in different ways and with different perspectives (and as new people come in and ask about "the same old things" in slightly different ways, sparking a new round of debate).

Beyond that, my best advice would be to just hang out on this Slack for a while. A lot of the big, useful ideas come up again and again as we explore them in different ways and with different perspectives (and as new people come in and ask about "the same old things" in slightly different ways, sparking a new round of debate).

The hard thing about doing a high-level summary (on, say, some web page) is that for most of the promising directions, the devil is in the details. What I've found is that knowing the current thinking around a certain topic isn't helpful (though it is interesting) unless I also know what foundational ideas those current thoughts are resting upon. You need to know the giant to stand on its shoulders.
